title: As Developers Close In, This Historic California AVA Is Standing Its Ground
summary:
The vines planted in the Contra Coast withstood Prohibition. Can they survive pressure from land developers?

The post As Developers Close In, This Historic California AVA Is Standing Its Ground appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
“It’s hard not to feel like a feather in a tornado sometimes,” says Morgan Twain-Peterson MW, owner and winemaker at Bedrock Wine Co. “We spend our lives trying to preserve and rehabilitate old vineyards, and the economic factors are so intense.”

Bedrock owns 10 acres of Evangelho Vineyard in Contra Costa County, California, and leases another 23 from Pacific Gas & Electric Company. Planted in the 1890s, some of the gnarly old vines grow out of 40-foot sand dunes beneath a canopy of power lines, surrounded by housing developments.

As grape growers in the area think about the future, it’s hard to ignore that the income from farming 10- to 20-acre parcels can’t compete with what a developer might pay for the same land. “It’s ironic that there’s so much pressure on these vineyards and that, in the midst of the current economics of the wine industry, we have a waiting list for people to buy fruit,” says Twain-Peterson.

Preserving these 130-year-old vines is a top priority for Contra Costa winegrowers. Matt and Erin Cline, co-owners of Three Wine Company, spearheaded a tidal restoration project that preserved the Joaquin José Vineyard, an upland oasis of Carignan vines planted in the 1880s. Cline Family Cellars, run by Matt’s relatives, has produced wine in the area for over 40 years.

Their Big Break Vineyard—named after a levee that broke in 1928—is “bordered by a railroad crossing and an Amazon fulfillment center,” says marketing manager Megan Cline. It’s a stark reminder of encroaching development.

The Contra Costa AVA is nested within the San Francisco Bay AVA, which in turn is within the Central Coast AVA. Vineyards’ silt, clay, and sand soils produce elegant red field blends from historic California varieties like Zinfandel, Mourvèdre (a.k.a. Mataro), and Carignan, plus whites primarily from Chardonnay and Sauvignon Blanc.

Kent Rosenblum, an early champion of California Zinfandel who was known as the King of Zin, first tasted Contra Costa fruit in the 1980s. “He’d buy the entire vineyard,” says his daughter, Shauna Rosenblum, the winemaker at Ridge Vineyards’ Lytton Springs winery. “Before long, we saw a renaissance due to the qualities of the field blends here.”

MOST WIDELY PLANTED RED-WINE GRAPES: Zinfandel, Mourvèdre, Carignan, Cabernet Sauvignon

MOST WIDELY PLANTED WHITE-WINE GRAPES: Chardonnay, Sauvignon Blanc,
Vermentino

Lovers of Contra Costa-grown wine can thank the gentle winds from the Sacramento-San Joaquin River Delta. As California’s Central Valley heats, a vacuum effect pulls cool air from the coast, and wind funnels through a gap in the Coastal Range. Even on hot days, the afternoons are windy.

“It’s prime weather for growing grapes, as it leads to a diurnal shift, where nights and mornings are cool,” explains Becky Bloomfield, president of the Contra Costa Winegrowers Association.

The tidal strait slows the water and, over thousands of years, has formed a vast sand-dune system along the San Joaquin River. “Brackish water and wind support a distinct salted plum or umami character in the wines,” Twain-Peterson says.

Bedrock makes a wine called “Areio e Vento e Amor” with a black-and-white photo of Frank Evangelho on the label. Evangelho, who tended to his namesake vineyard for half a century, died in 2018. The powerful yet refined blend of Zinfandel, Carignan, Mourvèdre, and Palomino is sourced from a gentle west-facing slope with only 302 vines.

“It’s our homage to him,” Twain-Peterson says. “It’s kind of its own thing.”

YEAR AVA ESTABLISHED: 2024
TOTAL ACREAGE: 167,146
PLANTED ACREAGE: Approx. 2,000
CLIMATE: Mediterranean climate with breezy afternoons and a hot, dry growing season
NUMBER OF WINERIES: 16

Bottles to Look For

Ridge Vineyards 2023 Sandy Lane Carignane (Contra Costa County)

This wine is a special bottling from a heritage site well over 100 years old and still producing fantastic fruit. Carignane has been used throughout history as a blending grape, but some long ago recognized its potential as a single-varietal expression. Aromas of youthful Red berries, florals, dried herbs, saline, and supple oak are integrated on the nose and on the palate, offering enjoyment now or throughout the next 6 to 10years if it lasts that long in your cellar without you consuming it. Editors’ Choice. 95 Points — Tonya Pitts

$38 Ridge Vineyards

Three 2024 Faux Pas Red Blend (Contra Costa County)

The chillable red wine is back in fashion although for some it has always been on the radar. This selection from Three Wine Co sources the best and expresses it as it should be – fresh, vibrant, with loads of red berries, savory herbs, and earth, while the palate repeats with a burst of dried cherry, pomegranate-strawberry, and star anise and mineral on the finish. Best Buy. 92 Points — T.P.

$27 Three Wine Company

Cline 2024 Grenache (Contra Costa County)

This wine is fresh with layers of red berries, dried florals, and toasty oak. The flavors on the palate are rich, red raspberry, red plum spearmint, dried coriander and mouthwatering tannins. 91 Points — T.P.

Inquire with Winery

Bet You Didn’t Know

Brentwood, a city in Contra Costa County that’s home to several family wineries, is known as the U-Pick Capital of the World—visitors can stock up on cherries, corn, tomatoes, and more.

title: 7 Cozy U.K. Pubs Where You Can Spend the Night
summary:
From the Cotswolds to the Scottish Isles, these historic pubs double as a cozy place to rest your head.

The post 7 Cozy U.K. Pubs Where You Can Spend the Night appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Imagine spending an evening in a traditional U.K. pub, sitting near a stone fireplace with a pint of English bitter, looking out at the countryside, and catching up with friends or family. When it’s time for bed, you simply head upstairs to a well-appointed guest room to spend the night. 

It sounds dreamy, but these types of stays could soon be a dying breed: In 2025, an average of one pub per day permanently closed in England and Wales, according to tax firm Ryan, which analyzed annual data from the U.K. government. In total, about 2,000 pubs have closed since 2020, according to data from the British Beer and Pub Association. 

With a stay in a charming pub, travelers can help preserve these cornerstones of local culture. “The best pub stays are small, intimate, and deeply rooted in their communities,” says Rachel Shoemaker, founder and senior travel advisor at Louisa White Travel. “I always look for pubs with walkable surroundings. I enjoy a short stroll to local shops, footpaths, and historic sites. The joy of a pub stay is being able to explore on foot and return to a welcoming bar at the end of the day.”

Here are seven charming and authentic pubs in the U.K. where travelers can eat, drink, and spend the night.

The Village Pub

Barnsley, England

With its oak-beamed ceilings, stone hearths, and numerous nooks and crannies, The Village Pub is a perfect place for a chat over a glass of regional English wine. “The Village Pub is very charming and located in one of my favorite parts of the Cotswolds,” says Ty Horn, managing director of the travel company Celebrated Experiences. 

At the bar, patrons can choose from a lineup of local gravity-fed ales, IPAs, or a classic pint of Guinness. The detailed wine list offers a selection from around the world, including Pinot Noir, Sauvignon Blanc, and Chardonnay from the surrounding Cotswolds. At meal time, guests can dig into a menu of upscale comfort food such as duck leg with truffle mash and an onion tart with walnut and blue cheese dressing.

Upstairs, there are six beautiful bedrooms sitting snugly in the eaves of the building. Each space is uniquely decorated and offers Egyptian cotton linens, plus an en suite bathroom with monsoon showers and rolltop tubs. To top it off, a traditional full English breakfast is included in the morning.

Isle of Raasay Distillery

Raasay, Scotland

On a windswept island off the coast of the Scottish Highlands sits the only distillery in Scotland where travelers can spend the night. Isle of Raasay Distillery makes both whisky and gin, so take a distillery tour, book a tasting, then spend the night right on the grounds.

Rooms are private and luxurious, with fluffy feather duvets, gas fireplaces, and en suite bathrooms. For those who’d like a more secluded stay, the property also offers five luxury cabins just up the hill from the distillery with designs inspired by traditional Scottish bothies, or stone cabins. The cabin’s full floor-to-ceiling windows are the perfect lookout point for drinking in Raasay’s natural beauty and the snow-dusted peaks of the Cuillin mountain range on the neighboring Isle of Skye.

All overnight guests get access to the distillery’s lounge and bar, where they can sample the gin and single-malt whisky made onsite, as well as local beer from nearby Cuillin Brewery on Skye. The distillery restaurant offers hot breakfast in the morning, as well as dinner and small plates of regional produce and locally caught seafood, laced with flavors from the distillery, like trout cured in its DRAAM single-malt whisky.

Coorie Inn

Muthill, Scotland

The charming red-brick exterior of Coorie Inn sits in the village center of Muthill, a historic Scottish town located in the Highland foothills. Inside, visitors are invited to snuggle up in a tufted armchair or couch as flames crackle in one of the rustic fireplaces, or dive into a match of chess or backgammon. “I love a cozy bar and lounge with books and board games,” says Shoemaker.

Up at the wooden plank bar, customers will find a selection of international wines and beers, plus local specialties like a variety of Scotch whiskys, including Glenturret 10-Year from the brand’s distillery, which is just a 10-minute drive away. 

Be sure to stop by the Coorie on a Sunday afternoon when the adjoining restaurant puts on classic roasts, featuring traditional dishes like stuffed pork loin, Scottish beef striploin, and Yorkshire pudding. When it’s time to turn in, the Coorie has six fresh and modern bedrooms upstairs, all with king-size beds (some of which can also be converted into two twins).

The Felin Fach Griffin

Felinfach Breton, Wales

The Griffin has all the trappings of a cozy local pub in spades: rustic beams, roaring fireplaces, comfy seats, and warm welcomes from both the bar staff and fellow customers. Situated on the outskirts of Wales’ stunning Bannau Brycheiniog National Park in Felinfach Breton, it’s a charming pit stop for travelers and hikers. “A good pub stay will usually highlight nearby walking trails and points of interest,” says Shoemaker. “When a pub encourages you to get outside and explore, that’s always a promising sign.”

Location aside, the Griffin is known for its memorable wine list. “We try to make wine an area where we are radically better” than other pubs, it declares on its menu. “We want our guests to discover new wines and wine areas.” Many of the staff have taken wine exams and participate in regular tastings with a handful of regional wine merchants with local expertise throughout Europe. The establishment focuses on serving about 20 wines by the glass and carafe to enable customers to try more varieties at each visit.

The pub says that the wine it serves is rarely available in stores, but if you like what you’ve tasted, The Griffin will sell you a bottle at cost, plus a markup of an extra pound or two. Enjoy your bottle of wine upstairs in one of the pub’s comfortable bedrooms with Bramley bath products and views of Wales’ Black Mountains.

The Falkland Arms

Great Tew, England

Located in Great Tew, one of the most picturesque villages in England’s verdant Cotswolds region, The Falkland Arms is essentially a platonic ideal of an English country pub. Entering is almost like stepping back in time: the establishment dates back to the 16th century and embraces its sense of history with its rustic interior design. Stone walls, low ceilings with thick wooden beams, flagstone floors, cozy seating nooks, and an inglenook fireplace all set the tone, while ceramic jugs and fairy lights suspended from the ceiling enhance the atmosphere. Menus are as traditional—and tasteful—as the decor, with upscale takes on pub classics, including sausage rolls, smoked salmon, and fish and chips with mushy peas.

The pub is associated with Wadworth Brewing, so you’ll find a substantial complement of Wadworth ales and craft beers from its nearby Folly Road Brewhouse, along with other seasonal cask and keg beers. There’s also a range of flavored gin and tonics on offer, featuring grapefruit- and rhubarb-flavored gins.

Quaint wooden staircases lead to the second and third floors, where seven snug bedrooms upstairs afford the option to extend the experience overnight. Rooms are dog-friendly and come with breakfast in the pub.

The Howard Arms

Ilmington, England

Another pub with a 400-year history is The Howard Arms, which sits on the village green in the heart of the Cotswolds town of Ilmington. Its flagstone floors, bay windows, leather armchairs, and log-burning fireplace all harken back to a simpler era. In the warmer months, guests can take advantage of the pub’s lush green lawn that’s dotted with picnic tables and umbrellas for al fresco enjoyment.

The extensive wine list features selections from all around the world. Also on tap are true English ales, beers, and ciders from local breweries, as well as gins from nearby purveyors like Cotswolds Distillery and Spirit of Ilmington. “When a pub celebrates what’s made nearby, the whole experience feels more authentic and memorable,” Shoemaker says. Local produce and house-baked breads with creative vegetarian dishes like mushroom crème brûlée complementing heartier fare, like beer-battered fish and chips and smash burgers.

The eight luxe bedrooms have the same authentic charms, with beamed ceilings and a mix of antique and modern furnishings, plus a hearty three-course breakfast service in the morning.

The Bull Charlbury

Charlbury, England

The Bull Charlbury claims to have been “the cornerstone of Charlbury for centuries.” Open since the 1500s, it has the history and charm to back up that statement. Sitting in the center of the town of Charlbury, the building is striking, with green ivy twining up an exterior made of yellow limestone bricks typical of the Cotswolds. The interior is the definition of rustic country charm, with multiple vast fireplaces, well-worn armchairs, tall taper candles, and simple wooden accents.

Pull up a stool or relax on a sofa and sip hyper-local beers and ciders from regional breweries like Hawkstone, as well as U.K. companies like Verdant Brewing Co. and Guinness. The Bull is also known for its culinary prowess, and its specialty is open-fire cooking—local lamb, beef, pork, and locally-grown vegetables are all roasted over the enormous wood-fire grill. As such, Horn says he regularly sends his clients touring in the region to The Bull for lunch and dinner.

For overnight guests, the pub’s ten bedrooms are understated and chic, with warm plaster walls, freestanding tubs, and super-king-size beds. It also offers stays in a two-bedroom former bakery around the corner that has been renovated into a picturesque guesthouse, aptly renamed Loaf Cottage.

title: South America Wants a Bigger Seat at the Sparkling Table
summary:
At Sip of South America 2026, producers and importers argued the region’s wines are overdue for global attention. Here's everything we learned at the event.

The post South America Wants a Bigger Seat at the Sparkling Table appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
On Tuesday, May 12, hundreds of wine professionals and consumers descended upon Halo 28 in New York City as Wine Enthusiast held its fourth annual Sip of South America. The event kicked off with a panel discussion hosted by Wine Enthusiast’s Tasting Director Anna-Christina Cabrales and WE Writer-at-Large Jesica Vargas on the state—and future—of South American winemaking.

Cabrales and Vargas were joined by three prominent figures in the South American wine scene: Oscar Garcia Moncada, wine and spirits director at NYC’s 67 Wine & Spirits; Jonathan Charnay, co-owner and beverage director at Nuyores, a South American restaurant in the West Village; and Cody Stephenson, Southern Hemisphere portfolio manager at Skurnik Wines & Spirits. Together, the panelists discussed the enormous diversity of South American wine—from light whites to hearty reds—and shared meaningful insights on the future of the industry.

One definite bright spot? Bubbles.

After the panel wrapped up, the floodgates opened, and attendees had the opportunity to taste wines from 39 wineries across Argentina, Brazil, Chile, and Uruguay—with each wine pulling a score of at least 90 points. 

Here are the biggest takeaways from the event.

Diversity Is Leading the Charge

South American winemakers are continuing to experiment with different styles of wine—and consumers are responding. 

“What’s resonating in South America is the diversity of wines being made here,” said Moncada. “People want more transparency. People want wines that are more food-friendly, vegan-friendly, and more agriculturally conscious.”

Charnay echoed these sentiments with observations from his restaurant. “The regions—I mean, South America in general—people don’t know how gigantic it is,” he said. “It’s immense, and there’s so much to discover: so many microregions, microclimates, and a lot of high-quality wines.”

Across the board, panelists noted that consumers are gravitating towards lighter, fresher styles of wine over the region’s time-honored tannic, high-alcohol wines. That being said, there’s still room on the table for the traditional styles of wine that many associate with South America. 

“We’re seeing right now that old wines of South America, the wines that gave the region its reputation, those wines have never been better,” said Vargas, in reference to its rich, oaked Malbecs and Cabernet Sauvignons.

Stephenson agreed, while also noting that these varieties do have the potential to adapt to trends. “Those wines are important for a reason, there’s such history, but I think the styles of those wines are changing,” he said. “Wines that were once aged in 50% or 100% new oak are [now in] neutral barrels or concrete eggs. I think winemakers are focusing on pick dates, they’re focusing on organic and biodynamic farming practices, and that’s having an effect on the wines.”

The Future Is Farming

As consumers turn towards more sustainable styles of wine, winemakers are figuring out how to meet the moment, doubling down on environmentally conscious farming and minimalistic winemaking practices.

“If producers can focus on organics, cleanliness, and making wines that are just very stable, the category of natural wouldn’t matter,” said David Koebley, national sales manager at Bodega Santa Julia, who worries that the term “natural” can confuse consumers. “‘Organic’ is more important. It can be defined.”

Koebley continued by describing Santa Julia’s longstanding commitment to sustainability and organic farming, which provides an easier transition to low-additive winemaking: “When you focus on quality [farming], then [the wines] naturally carry beautiful, wild complexity that you get from the vineyard.”

Cabrales also expressed interest in wines that allow the farming to speak for itself. 

“I want to see the fruit without makeup,” she said, adding that using neutral aging vessels can be a key factor. “Because of the way that producers are paying attention to the fruit, we’re really getting a good sense of terroir, and we want that to be fully expressed. That pure sensibility in the wines is really starting to resonate with consumers.”

South American Sparklers Continue to Shine

Sparkling wines from Brazil and Argentina are riding the popularity wave of lighter, fresher wines to great success. 

At the Santa Julia table, tasters came for the enticingly colorful labels but stayed for La Mantis. Made from 100% Chardonnay, this pét-nat opens with caramel aromas that lead into a fresh palate full of florals, citrus, and acidity.

Across the room, the energy surrounding the Brazilian bubbles’ table was as exuberant as the bottles themselves. There, attendees could enjoy Salton’s Intenso Brut Rosé, a lively sparkling rosé with just a smidge of residual sugar and an abv clocking in at 11.5%.

“It’s good for breakfast, lunch, and dinner…when you wake up and when you go to sleep,” said Sabrina Norbert, brand ambassador for the Salton estate. However hyperbolic, her sentiment stands: These are wines that are persuasively enjoyable and appropriate for any occasion.

Storytelling Is Driving Consumer Engagement

Throughout the entire event, one point of discussion remained consistent.

“It’s storytelling,” said Stephenson, when asked about the most important detail for selling wine to buyers. “We’re humans, and people want to connect with someone.”

Other panelists, winemakers, and attendees agreed, emphasizing that shifting the focus to the story of the wine and the humans involved can help engage drinkers with South American wines. 

“Make it real, share the humility of wine, share the stories, and help people understand that it’s just families who are farming,” said Koebley. “It’s not an unobtainable product that you can’t understand, [and] it shouldn’t be inaccessible for any generation or for any level of income.”

Looking Ahead

Anyone who walked through the doors of Sip of South America could sense the room’s inspiring energy and sense of optimism. 

As for Vargas, her vision is simple: “I hope that people keep trying and exploring South American wine. There are so many styles and different grape varieties. There’s something for everyone.”

title: How Viña Salceda Is Redefining Rioja Wine
summary:
As Spain’s most iconic wine region, Rioja is home to producers with decades of experience crafting complex, age-worthy wines primarily from Tempranillo. But with so much international recognition and market demand pegged to a single grape varietal, it can be tempting for producers and drinkers alike to characterize all of Rioja through a single, oversimplified

The post How Viña Salceda Is Redefining Rioja Wine appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
How Viña Salceda Is Redefining Rioja Wine

As Spain’s most iconic wine region, Rioja is home to producers with decades of experience crafting complex, age-worthy wines primarily from Tempranillo. But with so much international recognition and market demand pegged to a single grape varietal, it can be tempting for producers and drinkers alike to characterize all of Rioja through a single, oversimplified lens.

Capitalizing on and championing the nuance in Rioja’s subzones, as well as the remarkable potential for exceptional winemaking with other grape varietals, is no small feat.

But for the past 50 years, Viña Salceda has set the bar for winemaking in the region that respects tradition while embracing innovation and diversity, a difficult balance spearheaded primarily by winemaker David González. 

“At Viña Salceda, we highlight the diversity and quality of Rioja, positioning ourselves as a historic winery producing contemporary wines,” says González, who brings more than 30 years of experience making exceptional wines in Rioja to Viña Salceda.

“Our approach is rooted in technical expertise and a broad understanding of the region, rather than a purely emotional attachment to a single terroir.”

Two Distinct Subzones with Ample Nuance

Instead of relying on a formulaic, “one-size-fits-all” approach to winemaking, Gonzalez and his team honor and celebrate the variety of terroirs, climates, soil types, and grape varieties present within Rioja, primarily in the remote subzones of La Sonsierra and Alto Najerilla. 

“[Our] approach reflects a general trend in Rioja towards a more detailed understanding of vineyard origin and diversity,” says Gonzalez. “The differentiation between subzones reinforces a vision of the appellation that is more site-specific and less homogeneous.”

In Alto Najerilla, altitudes can reach between 500 and 600 meters. The team here focuses mainly on farming Garnacha, Viura, and Tempranillo Blanco. The continental climate produces grapes with marked acidity, bringing exceptional freshness to the wines. Here, González has played a pioneering role in the recovery of old vineyards in this subzone, having discovered vines that are over 70 years old.

“The recovery of old vineyards responds to a commitment to preserving viticultural heritage and seeking the most authentic expression of the territory, to achieve greater depth, freshness, and personality,” he explains. “Each site is worked individually to clearly express its identity, freshness, and balance, even in particularly challenging vintages.”

In contrast, the La Sonsierra subzone, home to Viña Salceda’s winery, ranges from 400 to 700 meters in altitude and is known for producing some of the finest Tempranillo-based wines. This south-facing area benefits from strong sun exposure and a Mediterranean climate combined with altitude. Alongside Tempranillo, Gonzalez also works with Graciano and Viura.

These grapes, from both young and old vines, provide structure, volume, and aromatic complexity, resulting in wines with depth and ageing potential.

Tradition and Contemporary Winemaking Coincide

“Our wines aim to strike a balance between classic Rioja and a more contemporary style, positioning Viña Salceda between traditional Rioja and what is often referred to as the ‘New Rioja,’” says Gonzalez. This progressive view on what Rioja can represent on the international stage has empowered Viña Salceda to move beyond a sole focus on Tempranillo, incorporating grape varieties such as Garnacha, Graciano, Viura, and Tempranillo Blanco. 

As a result, the winery’s portfolio is structured into two main ranges that balance Rioja’s tradition with a contemporary vision: Gama Atempo, which includes Crianzas, Reservas, and Gran Reservas wines made from high-quality grapes sourced from different subzones, often in cooler locations, as well as Gama Parajes, which focuses on expressing the diversity of the territory, highlighting the unique identity of specific sites.

This range emphasizes the singularity of the terroirs within Rioja, and results in distinctive and character-driven wines that convey the true essence of the region.

For Viña Salceda, one of the most important aspects of championing subzones lies in translating the nuances of those regions into each bottle of wine, as opposed to trying to mold them into a drinker’s predetermined, textbook understanding of Rioja.

This ethos reflects a growing trend in Rioja toward a more precise understanding of origin, where regions, villages, and specific vineyards increasingly take center stage. “We prioritize freshness, reduce the influence of oak, and seek elegance while allowing the authenticity of the terroir to shine through,” explains Gonzalez. “We aim for wines defined by aromatic precision, balance, and a clear expression of their origin.”

title: Is Macvin du Jura the Next ‘IYKYK’ Glass?
summary:
Winemakers in the Jura use leftover grape skins and fresh grape juice to make this sweet sipper. Wine nerds can't get enough.

The post Is Macvin du Jura the Next ‘IYKYK’ Glass? appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
A sweet fortified wine might be the next cult pour—the “IYKYK glass”—for industry pros and collectors fascinated by the Jura. 

Pay no mind to Jura’s small size; the tiny French wine region is a staple in New York‘s most influential wine bars, and produces a range of styles including sparkling Crémant, still wines, and more oxidative wines aged under flor. And lately, that already booming interest is expanding, with Macvin du Jura appearing under the dessert and sweet wine category on the menus of buzzy wine bars like Lei, the Four Horsemen, Stars, Entre Nous, and Gigi’s.

Macvin’s appearance on by-the-glass menus is thanks to a renewed enthusiasm for sweet wines, particularly among younger drinkers. According to a 2025 survey from the Wine Market Council, Gen Z and millennial drinkers included sweet styles among their preferred wines.

“Sweet wines are making a humongous comeback. We’re seeing a renaissance right now,” says Cameron Philip, sommelier and managing partner at Crybaby, a wine and cocktail bar focused on Alpine regions—including the Jura—scheduled to open this summer in the Lower East Side.

Philip adds that Macvin, like its popular and oxidative cousin Vin Jaune, is a unique traditional wine from the Jura, available only in limited quantities and very in demand for its idiosyncrasy. “Macvin is a little obscure, it’s interesting, and it’s a cultural practice that has existed for a long time,” he says.

Macvin du Jura is a vin de liqueur, made by blending grape juice with grape spirit (called “marc” or “eau de vie”) distilled from the skins and seeds of grapes after pressing at harvest. It can be made from any of the Jura’s five permitted varieties, most commonly as Macvin Blanc from Chardonnay and Savagnin, but sometimes as Macvin Rouge or Rosé from Trousseau, Poulsard, and Pinot Noir, when the fruit is macerated in the eau de vie to help extract color.

“In most wine regions, the grape skins are just something you discard after pressing,” says Marin Fumey, winemaker at Fumey-Chatelain. “But in the Jura, they were always reused to add value and make a nice sweet wine.”

Rules of Engagement

Long before Macvin du Jura was standardized as an appellation in 1991, it was a cooked wine, combined with aromatics and spices, and every winemaker had their own special recipe. Margaret of Flanders, Duchess of Burgundy in the 14th and 15th century, was supposedly fond of this proto-Macvin and gave it the name “Le Galant.”

Under the appellation rules, both the marc and the juice must come from the producer’s own grapes. “For Macvin you need to have healthy grapes for the eau de vie and the juice, and you need to be really cautious to avoid oxidation of the skins,” says Victoria Morin, winemaker at Domaine de la Touraize.

Vignerons in the Jura can distill their own marc, but most take their grape skins to a central distillery in Nevy-sur-Seille, just south of the scenic village of Château-Chalon. Others employ the services of a “bouilleur ambulant,” a mobile distillery that travels to the wineries (a service that has all but disappeared in France).

After distillation, the marc must be aged in oak for a minimum of 14 months. It’s then blended together with grape juice from the current harvest. “Macvin is one-third marc and two-thirds juice,” Morin explains. After it’s blended, the resulting mixture must then also be aged in oak for at least 14 months. At Domaine de la Touraize, it is aged for two years. “The longer it’s aged, the better,” Morin adds. 

Fumey concurs that extended aging is the key to achieving high-quality Macvin. “The longer you age it, the less you feel the alcohol. The alcohol is still there, but after long aging, it’s better integrated,” he says.

With age comes a creamy texture and more complexity, with aromas of dried fruit and spices. “There are very interesting botanical notes in the background,” Fumey observes. “A bit of mint, a bit of green tea.” 

Because the marc and the juice are from different harvests, Macvin never ​​carries a vintage on the label, but keep an eye out—some producers, like Domaine de la Touraize, will note on the back label the different years of the marc and juice.

Before and After

Traditionally, a glass of Macvin is a preferred way to start an evening in the Jura, but it functions as an excellent dessert wine as well. “After a night of drinking dry wine, it’s nice to drink something a bit sweeter, that brings you extra energy,” Fumey says. “Macvin is about 17 percent alcohol, which can be a lot as an aperitif, but it’s not that much as a digestif.”

At Crybaby, Philip also recommends ending the evening with Macvin du Jura. “Experiencing that spectrum—from sparkling to white to rosé to red to sweet—is so important,” he says, and wines from the Jura can deliver that entire spectrum, from the first sip to the last sweet drop.

title: This Fizzy, Citrusy Gin Drink Is Sunshine in a Glass
summary:
Carrot juice, fresh citrus, and aquafaba join forces in the summery Clementine Fizz cocktail. Here's how to make the bright gin drink at home.

The post This Fizzy, Citrusy Gin Drink Is Sunshine in a Glass appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
“California citrus is the absolute best!” exclaims Matt Duggan, general manager of West Hollywood’s A.O.C. Wine Bar, founded by Chef Suzanne Goin and restaurateur Caroline Styne.

Earlier this year, when a bumper crop of “particularly fantastic” tangerines arrived, the bar immediately started experimenting with drink ideas. “They’re less muscular than oranges, more delicate, so it felt like it lent itself to a gin drink,” Duggan recalls.

Tangerine juice is less sweet than orange juice. Coupling it with carrot syrup “brings sweetness and a vegetal quality,” Duggan says, and boosts the cocktail to a more vivid hue. “It’s that one layer that bumps it up and makes it amazing.”

If you’re inspired by a gorgeous bunch of carrots at the greenmarket, by all means juice your own. But Duggan says grabbing a bottle from a juice shop works well, too. “We understand that juicing your own carrots is a lot of work,” he says. “Do so only if you’re the kind of culinary masochist who just needs to reach and feel that pain, or if it scores you big points at the dinner party. Perhaps both.”

Clementine Fizz

Ignacio Murillo, head barman, A.O.C. Wine Bar, West Hollywood, CA

Ingredients

2 ounces London Dry-style gin
1 ounce lemon juice
1 1⁄2 ounces tangerine or clementine juice
3⁄4 ounce carrot-turmeric syrup*
3⁄4 ounce aquafaba** or 1⁄2 ounce egg whites
2–3 dashes orange bitters
1 ounce soda water
Edible flower or citrus peel (tangerine or orange), for garnish

Directions

In a shaker, combine all ingredients except soda water. Dry shake (no ice) to emulsify the aquafaba or egg whites. Add ice and shake again to chill. Strain into a Collins glass over fresh ice. Top up, with soda. Garnish with edible flower or citrus peel. If preferred, garnish with extra bitters. “If you’ve managed a nice froth, then a stain of the bitters can look cool and bring some aromatic excitement,” Duggan says.

*To make carrot-turmeric syrup, mix 4 ounces each of carrot juice and white sugar and 1⁄4 teaspoon ,turmeric powder in a saucepan. Simmer over medium heat for about 10 minutes, stirring until sugar dissolves. Remove from heat and let cool. Makes enough syrup for six drinks.

**Aquafaba is the starchy liquid leftover from soaking chickpeas or other legumes, often used as a vegan alternative to egg whites. For this drink, Duggan suggests purchasing aquafaba powder and mixing it with warm water, but you can also use the brine from a can of chickpeas.

title: My Dad Was My Wine Mentor. Now, I Collect for My Kids
summary:
Wine collecting isn’t really about building a cellar. It’s about taking something that naturally evolves over time and tying it to the people you love.

The post My Dad Was My Wine Mentor. Now, I Collect for My Kids appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
There’s a moment when a wine collection stops being about what’s in the bottle and starts becoming about who you’ll share it with. For me, that realization didn’t happen all at once. It crept in over time, somewhere between collecting bottles for myself and realizing I was quietly, yet purposefully, collecting for my kids.

When my son and daughter were born (2010 and 2012 respectively), I started setting aside wines from those vintages. At the time, it felt like a thoughtful gesture, maybe even a little romantic. But now, with them almost 14 and 16, those bottles feel like something more. It’s not just a case of wine on a shelf for each of them any longer; they’re placeholders for moments that haven’t happened yet. 

If I’m being honest, my dad is the reason I think this way about collecting. He’s the one who introduced me to wine (and maybe a little bit younger than he should have, but that’s another story.) There wasn’t some formal sit-down where he taught me how to swirl and sniff. It was simpler than that…It was about food on the table and an aged bottle of Riesling he was enjoying with it. Or a wicker-based Chianti that he and my grandpa were enjoying over some freshly made pasta and gravy. Sure, there were probably a couple of early (supervised!) sips along the way. But it wasn’t about “drinking” and partying it up. It was about paying attention to what was in the glass and talking about all the sensory experiences that we were noticing. Wondering why one wine tasted different from another. 

Even before I could fully understand it, I could feel that wine meant something more to him. Eventually, it started to mean something more to me, too. That’s the part that I think gets overlooked by many. You don’t fall in love with wine because someone hands you a great bottle that is worth hundreds of dollars… although that certainly doesn’t suck.  You become enthralled with it through moments, meaningful conversations, and emotions. And more often than not, those moments happen with a wine mentor. For me, that someone was my dad.

Tasting Together

Some of my favorite memories with my father are tied to bottles we’ve shared over the years—wines from Burgundy, the Rhône, and Napa Valley. We’ve opened bottles from Gevrey-Chambertin (my epiphany wine that we shared as a family) and Gigondas that turned into long conversations, debates, and the occasional friendly argument about what we were tasting. What started as him teaching me slowly turned into something more balanced as our wine knowledge and passion reached the same level. There are times now where I’ll offer him the first glass of wine at an occasion, and he will ask me what it is. My response is almost always… “You tell me, pops.” And more often than not, he still gets it right!

Just like sports, wine helped us connect in a way that didn’t feel forced. It created space to spend time together, to share something, to learn from each other. And somewhere along the way, it became our thing. Now, as a father myself, I think about that more than I probably expected to. My kids aren’t there yet, although a supervised and educated sip may or may not still be enjoyed. But those birth-year bottles are still resting, evolving slowly in the cellar, just waiting for the right opportunity to enjoy them with my kiddos. I already find myself thinking about the day we’ll open them and taste what has been collected on their respective shelves. Not because I expect some life-changing tasting note (although that would be awesome!), but because I know precisely what that moment can represent.

In Stock | $5,595

Shop Now

Twenty-plus years in a properly stored cellar, protected from heat, light, vibration, oxygen, and odors, can turn a bottle into something complex and layered. But even more than that, it turns it into a shared experience that’s been building over time. And the truth is, they might not love it right away. Aged wine isn’t always the most popular wine for new drinkers, as it doesn’t typically have that big concentrated fruit or power. Most often, bottle age will bring about elegance, refinement, and some form of earthy or dried fruit notes. But that’s not really the point. The point is the realization that what’s in the glass has been there, waiting, for as long as they’ve been alive. 

Maybe it turns into a shared interest and maybe, if you’re lucky, it turns into something bigger and more meaningful. Trips to wine regions, walking vineyards together, tasting through new wines. Maybe one day you’re sitting in a tasting room in Napa Valley or the Finger Lakes with your kids, or wandering through a village in Burgundy, picking out bottles not just to drink, but to save for their own future moments.

I’ve seen how that plays out over time with my dad. The conversations are different and the dynamic has shifted. There are moments where I’m still learning from him, and others where I can challenge him and expand his knowledge and appreciation. It’s not about who’s right, it’s always about the experience of figuring it out together.

The Right Bottles with the Right People

Then, there’s the part no one really talks about when they start collecting: you’re just not going to be able to drink everything you’ve collected over the years. At some point, you realize the cellar you have been building over decades wasn’t just for you. These bottles are meant to be opened, shared, and, in some cases, passed on. Some of the best bottles I’ve had weren’t the most expensive or the rarest… they were the ones opened at the right time with the right people.

If I’m lucky, collecting doesn’t stop with me. Maybe one day it’s not just me and my kids at the dinner table with a fabulous bottle of wine, but my dad, too (and my mom, of course). Three generations, popping open one of my kids’ birth-year wines, and having a spirited and passionate conversation not only about the wine, but about the year on the label and all of the wonderful memories that came along with it.

At the end of the day, wine collecting isn’t really about building a cellar. It’s about taking something that naturally evolves over time and tying it to the people you care about most. And if you do it right, those bottles don’t just age well… they can bring your family closer together than you may have ever imagined, one glass at a time.

title: In Defense of the Vodka Martini
summary:
While my taste in wine has evolved over the years, my martini preference has not, much to the surprise of my Wine Enthusiast colleagues.

The post In Defense of the Vodka Martini appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Wine is nice, but when I want to dance with oblivion, nothing will satisfy my desire like a Grey Goose martini straight up with three blue cheese olives, if you have them. Not dirty; more like the Diet Coke of dirty, with just enough olive brine to keep the vodka and vermouth in the spotlight.

My martini order is a point of controversy among my beverage-industry peers who are willing to die on the hill of purism. The original martini, after all, calls for gin. “Gin is complex.” “Gin is an art.” “Gin is superior in every way.” These are just some of the words of mass destruction I’ve heard in response to my vodka preference. I agree with the sentiment and respect their passion. I often enjoy gin in cocktails—just not in martinis.

The first time I ordered a martini was many years ago at a Greenwich Village dive bar. Back then, I bought $15 bottles of wine from the bin next to the checkout and ordered wine by the glass in basement bars, served warm and poured to the rim. But that night was different, and I needed something more. I was two years into my post-college working life, feeling like a young racehorse stuck behind the gate, running in place. I needed relief, escape, transcendence. So when the server came to our table, I repeated the order I had grown up hearing my mom say at countless restaurants. Anything my parents did seemed sophisticated at the time, and now that I was a grown-up with a grown-up job and grown-up stress, it was time to do grown-up things like drink Grey Goose martinis straight up with three olives.

While my taste in wine has evolved over the years, my martini preference has not, much to the surprise of my Wine Enthusiast colleagues. I recently went on a martini crawl of Manhattan with our spirits reviewer, Kara Newman. Naturally, she’s Team Gin, but not annoying about it, so she was the perfect guide. My mission: Drink the best gin martinis—in Midtown, because I am a suburbanite with kids who doesn’t want to take the train home late at night (sure, another reason to judge my tastes)—to see if they could convert me.

I sampled four distinct gin martinis: olive brine-rinsed at Hawksmoor; cucumber-and pink-pepper-infused with an oyster on the side at The Tusk Bar; double-chilled and unfiltered at Seed Library; olive bitters and rosemary at Drink. The closest I came to joining Team Gin was at Hawksmoor, where they merely rinsed the tin with olive brine before mixing in Sipsmith gin and very dry vermouth, so it was only somewhat dirty. They gave me extra olives on the side, bathing in brine, so I could dirty up the experience to my liking. The olives were meaty and velvety, which the gin cut through like a new dance partner waiting for their chance. This gin martini sang a new harmony to a tune I’ve heard before—floral notes, briny at first, with a citrusy aftertaste.

One of the many incredible bartenders I met that evening said something that stuck with me. She prefers gin martinis because “I like to think about what I’m drinking.” And that’s exactly my point, too. Despite the undeniable beauty and complexity of gin and all the nuanced notes it delivers, I still prefer a vodka martini. Because sometimes, you don’t want to think. Sometimes, you just need the comfort of what is familiar. A drink that warms your body and eases your mind with a song you already know the words to. So for now, it’s vodka on repeat.
